questions.questionText: The shortest distance between the lines $\frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z-2}{-3}$ and $\frac{x+2}{2}=\frac{y-6}{4}=\frac{z-5}{-5}$ is:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For the two lines

$$ \frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z-2}{-3} $$

and

$$ \frac{x+2}{2}=\frac{y-6}{4}=\frac{z-5}{-5}, $$

we use the NCERT formula for the shortest distance between two skew lines:

$$ \text{Shortest distance}= \frac{\left|(\vec{a_2}-\vec{a_1})\cdot(\vec{b_1}\times \vec{b_2})\right|}{\left|\vec{b_1}\times \vec{b_2}\right|} $$

where:

  • $\vec{a_1}$ is a point on the first line

  • $\vec{a_2}$ is a point on the second line

  • $\vec{b_1}, \vec{b_2}$ are direction vectors of the two lines

Step 1: Identify points and direction vectors

From the first line,

$$ \frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z-2}{-3} $$

a point on it is

$$ A(4,3,2) $$

and its direction vector is

$$ \vec{b_1}=\hat{i}+2\hat{j}-3\hat{k} $$

From the second line,

$$ \frac{x+2}{2}=\frac{y-6}{4}=\frac{z-5}{-5} $$

a point on it is

$$ B(-2,6,5) $$

and its direction vector is

$$ \vec{b_2}=2\hat{i}+4\hat{j}-5\hat{k} $$

Step 2: Find $\vec{AB}$

$$ \vec{AB}=B-A=(-2-4,\ 6-3,\ 5-2)=(-6,3,3) $$

So,

$$ \vec{AB}=-6\hat{i}+3\hat{j}+3\hat{k} $$

Step 3: Find $\vec{b_1}\times \vec{b_2}$

$$ \vec{b_1}\times \vec{b_2} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k}\\ 1 & 2 & -3\\ 2 & 4 & -5 \end{vmatrix} $$

Expanding,

$$ = \hat{i}\left(2(-5)-(-3)(4)\right) -\hat{j}\left(1(-5)-(-3)(2)\right) +\hat{k}\left(1\cdot 4-2\cdot 2\right) $$

$$ = \hat{i}(-10+12)-\hat{j}(-5+6)+\hat{k}(4-4) $$

$$ = 2\hat{i}-\hat{j}+0\hat{k} $$

So,

$$ \vec{b_1}\times \vec{b_2}=(2,-1,0) $$

Its magnitude is

$$ \left|\vec{b_1}\times \vec{b_2}\right| =\sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+0^2} =\sqrt{5} $$

Step 4: Find scalar triple product

$$ \vec{AB}\cdot(\vec{b_1}\times \vec{b_2}) =(-6,3,3)\cdot(2,-1,0) $$

$$ = (-6)(2)+(3)(-1)+(3)(0) $$

$$ = -12-3=-15 $$

So,

$$ \left| \vec{AB}\cdot(\vec{b_1}\times \vec{b_2}) \right|=15 $$

Step 5: Find shortest distance

$$ d=\frac{15}{\sqrt{5}}=3\sqrt{5} $$

Hence, the shortest distance is

$$ \boxed{3\sqrt{5}} $$

So, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option C}} $$

questions.questionText: The area of the region $\left\{(x, y): x^2-8 x \leq y \leq-x\right\}$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For the region

$$ \{(x,y): x^2-8x \le y \le -x\}, $$

the lower curve is

$$ y=x^2-8x $$

and the upper curve is

$$ y=-x. $$

To find the area between the curves, we first find their points of intersection.

Set

$$ x^2-8x=-x $$

$$ x^2-7x=0 $$

$$ x(x-7)=0 $$

So, the curves intersect at

$$ x=0 \quad \text{and} \quad x=7. $$

Now, on the interval $[0,7]$, the line $y=-x$ lies above the parabola $y=x^2-8x$.

Hence, the required area is

$$ \text{Area}=\int_0^7 \left[(-x)-(x^2-8x)\right]dx. $$

Simplify the integrand:

$$ (-x)-(x^2-8x)=-x-x^2+8x=7x-x^2. $$

Therefore,

$$ \text{Area}=\int_0^7 (7x-x^2)\,dx. $$

Now integrate:

$$ \int (7x-x^2)\,dx=\frac{7x^2}{2}-\frac{x^3}{3}. $$

Applying the limits from $0$ to $7$:

$$ \text{Area}=\left[\frac{7x^2}{2}-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^7 $$

$$ =\left(\frac{7\cdot 49}{2}-\frac{343}{3}\right)-0 $$

$$ =\frac{343}{2}-\frac{343}{3} $$

Taking LCM $6$:

$$ =\frac{1029-686}{6} =\frac{343}{6}. $$

Hence, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{\frac{343}{6}} $$

So, the correct option is A.

questions.questionText: Let C be a circle having centre in the first quadrant and touching the $x$-axis at a distance of 3 units from the origin. If the circle $C$ has an intercept of length $6 \sqrt{3}$ on $y$-axis, then the length of the chord of the circle C on the line $x-y=3$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Since the circle touches the $x$-axis at a point which is $3$ units from the origin, the point of contact is $(3,0)$.

The centre lies in the first quadrant and the radius is perpendicular to the tangent at the point of contact. So, the centre must be of the form $(3,r)$, where $r$ is the radius.

The circle has an intercept of length $6\sqrt{3}$ on the $y$-axis. This means the chord cut by the line $x=0$ has length $6\sqrt{3}$.

For a circle of radius $r$, if the distance of a chord from the centre is $d$, then chord length is $2\sqrt{r^2-d^2}$.

Here, the distance from centre $(3,r)$ to the $y$-axis $(x=0)$ is $d=3$. So the intercept on the $y$-axis is

$$2\sqrt{r^2-3^2}=6\sqrt{3}$$

So,

$$\sqrt{r^2-9}=3\sqrt{3}\ \Rightarrow\ r^2-9=27\ \Rightarrow\ r^2=36\ \Rightarrow\ r=6$$

Hence, centre of the circle is $(3,6)$ and radius is $6$ units.

Now we need the length of the chord cut by the line $x-y=3$.

First find the perpendicular distance $p$ from the centre $(3,6)$ to the line $x-y-3=0$:

$$p=\frac{|1\cdot 3+(-1)\cdot 6-3|}{\sqrt{1^2+(-1)^2}}=\frac{|3-6-3|}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{6}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Now chord length on this line is

$P Q=2 \sqrt{6^2-p^2} \quad \therefore p=\frac{6}{\sqrt{2}}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{p}=\frac{|3-6-3|}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \mathrm{PQ}=2 \sqrt{36-\frac{36}{2}} \\ & =2 \times \frac{6}{\sqrt{2}}=6 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$$

questions.questionText: Let $S=\left\{z \in \mathbb{C}: z^2+\sqrt{6} i z-3=0\right\}$. Then $\sum\limits_{z \in S} z^8$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & z=\frac{-\sqrt{6} i \pm \sqrt{-6+12}}{2} \\ & =\frac{-\sqrt{6} i \pm \sqrt{6}}{2} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}( \pm 1-i)\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \alpha=\frac{-\sqrt{6}}{2}(1+\mathrm{i}), \beta=\frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}(1-\mathrm{i}) \\ & \alpha=\frac{-\sqrt{6}}{2} \times \sqrt{2} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} \pi / 4}, \beta=+\frac{\sqrt{6}}{2} \times \sqrt{2}-\mathrm{i} \pi / 4 \\ & \alpha=-\sqrt{3} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} \pi / 4}, \beta=+\sqrt{3} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{i} \pi / 4}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \alpha^8+\beta^8=81 \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} 2 \pi}+81 \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}(-2 \pi)} \\ & =81(1+1)=162\end{aligned}$

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 3 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 & \alpha \\ 0 & 1 & -1\end{array}\right]$ be a singular matrix. Let $f(x)=\int_0^x\left(\mathrm{t}^2+2 \mathrm{t}+3\right) \mathrm{dt}, x \in[1, \alpha]$. If M and m are respectively the maximum and the minimum values of $f$ in $[1, \alpha]$, then $3(\mathrm{M}-\mathrm{m})$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Since $A$ is a singular matrix, its determinant must be zero.

We have

$$ A=\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & -1\\ 2 & 1 & \alpha\\ 0 & 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$

So,

$$ |A|=0 $$

Now let us calculate the determinant by expanding along the first row:

$$ |A|=1\begin{vmatrix}1 & \alpha\\ 1 & -1\end{vmatrix} -3\begin{vmatrix}2 & \alpha\\ 0 & -1\end{vmatrix} +(-1)\begin{vmatrix}2 & 1\\ 0 & 1\end{vmatrix} $$

$$ =1[(1)(-1)-(\alpha)(1)]-3[(2)(-1)-(\alpha)(0)]-1[(2)(1)-(1)(0)] $$

$$ =(-1-\alpha)-3(-2)-2 $$

$$ =-1-\alpha+6-2 $$

$$ =3-\alpha $$

Since $|A|=0$,

$$ 3-\alpha=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=3 $$

Now,

$$ f(x)=\int_0^x (t^2+2t+3)\,dt $$

Integrating,

$$ f(x)=\left[\frac{t^3}{3}+t^2+3t\right]_0^x =\frac{x^3}{3}+x^2+3x $$

We need maximum and minimum values of $f(x)$ in $[1,\alpha]=[1,3]$.

Now check whether $f(x)$ is increasing or decreasing.

$$ f'(x)=x^2+2x+3 $$

$$ f'(x)=x^2+2x+3=(x+1)^2+2>0 \quad \text{for all } x $$

So $f(x)$ is strictly increasing on $[1,3]$.

Hence,

  • minimum value at $x=1$

  • maximum value at $x=3$

Now,

$$ m=f(1)=\frac{1}{3}+1+3=\frac{13}{3} $$

and

$$ M=f(3)=\frac{27}{3}+9+9=9+9+9=27 $$

Therefore,

$$ M-m=27-\frac{13}{3} =\frac{81-13}{3} =\frac{68}{3} $$

So,

$$ 3(M-m)=3\cdot \frac{68}{3}=68 $$

Hence the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{68} $$

So, the correct option is $\boxed{\text{Option B}}$.

questions.questionText: The value of the integral $\int\limits_{-1}^1\left(\frac{x^3+|x|+1}{x^2+2|x|+1}\right) \mathrm{d} x$ is equal to:
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.questionText:
Let $f: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be such that $f(x y)=f(x) f(y)$, for all $x, y \in \mathbf{R}$ and $f(0) \neq 0$. Let $g:[1, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be a differentiable function such that

$$ x^2 g(x)=\int_1^x\left(\mathrm{t}^2 f(\mathrm{t})-\operatorname{tg}(\mathrm{t})\right) d t $$

Then $g(2)$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We are given

$$ f(xy)=f(x)f(y)\quad \text{for all }x,y\in \mathbb{R}, $$

and

$$ f(0)\ne 0. $$

Also,

$$ x^2g(x)=\int_1^x \left(t^2f(t)-tg(t)\right)\,dt,\qquad x\ge 1. $$

We have to find $g(2)$.

Step 1: Use the condition on $f$

Given

$$ f(xy)=f(x)f(y) $$

for all real $x,y$.

Now put $x=0$ and any real $y$. Then

$$ f(0\cdot y)=f(0)f(y) $$

that is,

$$ f(0)=f(0)f(y). $$

Since $f(0)\ne 0$, we can divide by $f(0)$:

$$ f(y)=1 \quad \text{for all } y\in \mathbb{R}. $$

So,

$$ f(t)=1 \quad \text{for all } t. $$

Step 2: Substitute into the given integral equation

The equation becomes

$$ x^2g(x)=\int_1^x (t^2-tg(t))\,dt. $$

Step 3: Differentiate both sides

Differentiate with respect to $x$.

Left side:

$$ \frac{d}{dx}[x^2g(x)] = x^2g'(x)+2xg(x). $$

Right side, by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\int_1^x (t^2-tg(t))\,dt = x^2-xg(x). $$

Hence,

$$ x^2g'(x)+2xg(x)=x^2-xg(x). $$

So,

$$ x^2g'(x)+3xg(x)=x^2. $$

Divide by $x$ (since $x\ge 1$):

$$ xg'(x)+3g(x)=x. $$

Or,

$$ g'(x)+\frac{3}{x}g(x)=1. $$

Step 4: Solve the differential equation

This is a linear differential equation:

$$ g'(x)+\frac{3}{x}g(x)=1. $$

Its integrating factor is

$$ \mathrm{I.F.}=e^{\int \frac{3}{x}\,dx}=e^{3\ln x}=x^3. $$

Multiply both sides by $x^3$:

$$ x^3g'(x)+3x^2g(x)=x^3. $$

The left side is

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(x^3g(x)). $$

So,

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(x^3g(x))=x^3. $$

Integrate:

$$ x^3g(x)=\frac{x^4}{4}+C. $$

Thus,

$$ g(x)=\frac{x}{4}+\frac{C}{x^3}. $$

Step 5: Find the constant using the original equation

Put $x=1$ in

$$ x^2g(x)=\int_1^x (t^2-tg(t))\,dt. $$

Then

$$ 1^2g(1)=\int_1^1 (t^2-tg(t))\,dt=0. $$

So,

$$ g(1)=0. $$

Now use

$$ g(1)=\frac{1}{4}+C=0. $$

Hence,

$$ C=-\frac{1}{4}. $$

Therefore,

$$ g(x)=\frac{x}{4}-\frac{1}{4x^3}. $$

Step 6: Find $g(2)$

$$ g(2)=\frac{2}{4}-\frac{1}{4(2^3)} =\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{32} =\frac{16-1}{32} =\frac{15}{32}. $$

So the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{\frac{15}{32}}. $$

Hence, Option C.

questions.questionText: Let $x=9$ be a directrix of an ellipse E , whose centre is at the origin and eccentricity is $\frac{1}{3}$. Let $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, 0)$, $\alpha>0$, be a focus of E and AB be a chord passing through P . Then the locus of the mid point of AB is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Since the centre of the ellipse is at the origin and one directrix is $x=9$, the major axis is along the $x$-axis.

Let the ellipse be

$$ \frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1 $$

with eccentricity

$$ e=\frac{1}{3} $$

For an ellipse with major axis along the $x$-axis, the directrices are

$$ x=\pm \frac{a}{e} $$

Given one directrix is $x=9$, so

$$ \frac{a}{e}=9 $$

Since $e=\frac13$,

$$ a=9\cdot \frac13=3 $$

Also,

$$ c=ae=3\cdot \frac13=1 $$

So the focus $P(\alpha,0)$ is

$$ P=(1,0) $$

Hence $\alpha=1$.

Now,

$$ b^2=a^2(1-e^2)=9\left(1-\frac19\right)=9\cdot \frac89=8 $$

Therefore the ellipse is

$$ \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{8}=1 $$

Let the midpoint of chord $AB$ passing through the focus $(1,0)$ be $(h,k)$.

For the ellipse

$$ \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{8}=1 $$

the equation of the chord whose midpoint is $(h,k)$ is given by the NCERT midpoint formula:

$$ \frac{xx_1}{9}+\frac{yy_1}{8}=\frac{x_1^2}{9}+\frac{y_1^2}{8} $$

So here the chord with midpoint $(h,k)$ is

$$ \frac{xh}{9}+\frac{yk}{8}=\frac{h^2}{9}+\frac{k^2}{8} $$

Since this chord passes through the focus $(1,0)$, substitute $(x,y)=(1,0)$:

$$ \frac{h}{9}=\frac{h^2}{9}+\frac{k^2}{8} $$

Multiply by $72$:

$$ 8h=8h^2+9k^2 $$

So,

$$ 9k^2=8h-8h^2=8h(1-h) $$

Replacing $(h,k)$ by $(x,y)$ for the locus of the midpoint, we get

$$ 9y^2=8x(1-x) $$

Hence the required locus is

$$ \boxed{9y^2=8x(1-x)} $$

So the correct option is A.

questions.questionText: Let $f: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be defined as $f(x)=\frac{2 x^2-3 x+2}{3 x^2+x+3}$. Then $f$ is :
options:
both one-one and onto

one-one but not onto

onto but not one-one

neither one-one nor onto
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
To determine the nature of the given function, let us carefully analyze it for both the one-one (injective) and onto (surjective) properties. The function is given by:

$$f(x)=\frac{2 x^2-3 x+2}{3 x^2+x+3}$$

where the domain is the set of real numbers $\mathbf{R}$ and the codomain is also $\mathbf{R}$.

Checking if the function is one-one

A function $f: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ is said to be one-one if every element in the domain has a distinct image in the codomain. That is, if $f(x_1) = f(x_2)$, then we must have $x_1 = x_2$.

Let us check if we can find two different real values of $x$ that give the same value for $f(x)$. Let us set $f(x) = 1$ and solve for $x$:

$$\frac{2 x^2-3 x+2}{3 x^2+x+3} = 1$$

Cross-multiplying, we obtain:

$$2 x^2-3 x+2 = 3 x^2+x+3$$

Bringing all terms to one side, we get:

$$x^2 + 4x + 1 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula to find the roots:

$$x = \frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 4(1)(1)}}{2} = \frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{12}}{2} = -2 \pm \sqrt{3}$$

Since we found two distinct real values, $x_1 = -2 + \sqrt{3}$ and $x_2 = -2 - \sqrt{3}$, such that $f(x_1) = f(x_2) = 1$, the function is not one-one.

Checking if the function is onto

A function is onto if its range is equal to its codomain. Here, the codomain is $\mathbf{R}$. To find the range, let us set $f(x) = y$:

$$y = \frac{2 x^2-3 x+2}{3 x^2+x+3}$$

Cross-multiplying gives us:

$$y(3x^2 + x + 3) = 2x^2 - 3x + 2$$

$$3yx^2 + yx + 3y = 2x^2 - 3x + 2$$

Grouping the terms by the powers of $x$, we form a quadratic equation in $x$:

$$(3y - 2)x^2 + (y + 3)x + (3y - 2) = 0$$

Since $x$ is a real number, the discriminant ($D$) of this quadratic equation must be greater than or equal to zero ($D \geq 0$).

$$D = (y + 3)^2 - 4(3y - 2)(3y - 2) \geq 0$$

$$(y + 3)^2 - 4(3y - 2)^2 \geq 0$$

This is in the form of $a^2 - b^2 \geq 0$, which can be factored as $(a-b)(a+b) \geq 0$. Here, $a = y+3$ and $b = 2(3y-2) = 6y-4$:

$$[(y + 3) - (6y - 4)][(y + 3) + (6y - 4)] \geq 0$$

$$(-5y + 7)(7y - 1) \geq 0$$

To solve this inequality, we can multiply by $-1$ (which reverses the inequality sign):

$$(5y - 7)(7y - 1) \leq 0$$

The critical points are $y = \frac{1}{7}$ and $y = \frac{7}{5}$. Based on the inequality, $y$ must lie between these two values:

$$\frac{1}{7} \leq y \leq \frac{7}{5}$$

This means the range of the function is the closed interval $\left[ \frac{1}{7}, \frac{7}{5} \right]$.

Because the range is a small subset of the real numbers and not equal to the entire codomain $\mathbf{R}$, the function is not onto.

Conclusion:

Since $f(x)$ is neither one-one nor onto, the correct statement is given by Option D.

Option D

questions.questionText: The eccentricity of an ellipse $E$ with centre at the origin $O$ is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and its directrices are $x= \pm \frac{4 \sqrt{6}}{3}$. Let $\mathrm{H}: \frac{x^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}-\frac{y^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1$ be a hyperbola whose eccentricity is equal to the length of semi-major axis of E , and whose length of latus rectum is equal to the length of minor axis of E . Then the distance between the foci of H is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For the ellipse $E$, let its equation be

$$ \frac{x^2}{A^2}+\frac{y^2}{B^2}=1, \quad A>B $$

since its major axis is along the $x$-axis because the directrices are of the form $x=\text{constant}$.

Given eccentricity,

$$ e=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$

For an ellipse with major axis along the $x$-axis, the directrices are

$$ x=\pm \frac{A}{e} $$

We are given

$$ x=\pm \frac{4\sqrt{6}}{3} $$

So,

$$ \frac{A}{e}=\frac{4\sqrt{6}}{3} $$

Substituting $e=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$,

$$ A=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cdot \frac{4\sqrt{6}}{3} $$

$$ A=\frac{4\sqrt{18}}{6} =\frac{4\cdot 3\sqrt{2}}{6} =2\sqrt{2} $$

Thus the semi-major axis of ellipse $E$ is

$$ A=2\sqrt{2} $$

Now for the ellipse,

$$ B^2=A^2(1-e^2) $$

Since

$$ A^2=(2\sqrt{2})^2=8 $$

and

$$ e^2=\frac{3}{4} $$

we get

$$ B^2=8\left(1-\frac{3}{4}\right)=8\cdot \frac{1}{4}=2 $$

Hence

$$ B=\sqrt{2} $$

So the length of minor axis of $E$ is

$$ 2B=2\sqrt{2} $$

Now consider the hyperbola

$$ H:\frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1 $$

Its eccentricity is equal to the length of semi-major axis of $E$, so

$$ e_H=2\sqrt{2} $$

For a hyperbola,

$$ e_H^2=1+\frac{b^2}{a^2} $$

Thus,

$$ (2\sqrt{2})^2=1+\frac{b^2}{a^2} $$

$$ 8=1+\frac{b^2}{a^2} $$

$$ \frac{b^2}{a^2}=7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b^2=7a^2 $$

Also, the length of latus rectum of the hyperbola is equal to the length of minor axis of $E$.

For the hyperbola, length of latus rectum is

$$ \frac{2b^2}{a} $$

So,

$$ \frac{2b^2}{a}=2\sqrt{2} $$

$$ \frac{b^2}{a}=\sqrt{2} $$

Using $b^2=7a^2$,

$$ \frac{7a^2}{a}=\sqrt{2} $$

$$ 7a=\sqrt{2} $$

$$ a=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{7} $$

Now for the hyperbola,

$$ c=ae_H $$

So,

$$ c=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{7}\cdot 2\sqrt{2} =\frac{4}{7} $$

Distance between the foci $=2c$,

$$ 2c=\frac{8}{7} $$

Hence, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{\frac{8}{7}} $$

So, Option D is correct.

questions.questionText: If $\sin \left(\tan ^{-1}(x \sqrt{2})\right)=\cot \left(\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{1-x^2}\right), x \in(0,1)$, then the value of $x$ is:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let us solve

$$ \sin\left(\tan^{-1}(x\sqrt{2})\right)=\cot\left(\sin^{-1}\sqrt{1-x^2}\right), \qquad x\in(0,1) $$

step by step.

Step 1: Simplify the left-hand side

Let

$$ \theta=\tan^{-1}(x\sqrt{2}) $$

Then

$$ \tan\theta=x\sqrt{2} $$

We know that if $\tan\theta=\dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$, then we can take

  • opposite $=x\sqrt{2}$

  • adjacent $=1$

So hypotenuse is

$$ \sqrt{1+(x\sqrt{2})^2}=\sqrt{1+2x^2} $$

Hence,

$$ \sin\theta=\frac{x\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{1+2x^2}} $$

Therefore,

$$ \sin\left(\tan^{-1}(x\sqrt{2})\right)=\frac{x\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{1+2x^2}} $$

Step 2: Simplify the right-hand side

Let

$$ \phi=\sin^{-1}\sqrt{1-x^2} $$

Then

$$ \sin\phi=\sqrt{1-x^2} $$

Since $x\in(0,1)$, we have $\sqrt{1-x^2}\in(0,1)$, so $\phi$ is acute.

Now,

$$ \cos\phi=\sqrt{1-\sin^2\phi} = \sqrt{1-(1-x^2)} = \sqrt{x^2} = x $$

because $x>0$.

Therefore,

$$ \cot\phi=\frac{\cos\phi}{\sin\phi} =\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} $$

So,

$$ \cot\left(\sin^{-1}\sqrt{1-x^2}\right)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} $$

Step 3: Equate both sides

Now the given equation becomes

$$ \frac{x\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{1+2x^2}}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} $$

Since $x\in(0,1)$, we have $x\neq 0$, so divide both sides by $x$:

$$ \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{1+2x^2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} $$

Step 4: Cross multiply

$$ \sqrt{2}\,\sqrt{1-x^2}=\sqrt{1+2x^2} $$

Now square both sides:

$$ 2(1-x^2)=1+2x^2 $$

$$ 2-2x^2=1+2x^2 $$

$$ 1=4x^2 $$

$$ x^2=\frac{1}{4} $$

Since $x\in(0,1)$,

$$ x=\frac{1}{2} $$

Final Answer

$$ \boxed{\frac{1}{2}} $$

So, the correct option is Option A.

questions.questionText:
The sum of all possible values of $\theta \in[0,2 \pi]$, for which the system of equations :

$$ \begin{aligned} & x \cos 3 \theta-8 y-12 z=0 \\ & x \cos 2 \theta+3 y+3 z=0 \\ & x+y+3 z=0 \end{aligned} $$

has a non-trivial solution, is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc}\cos 3 \theta & -8 & -12 \\ \cos 2 \theta & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 & 3\end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \left|\begin{array}{ccc}\cos 3 \theta & -8 & -4 \\ \cos 2 \theta & 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|=0\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \left(C_1 \rightarrow C_1-C_2\right) \& C_2 \rightarrow C_2-C_3 \\ & \left|\begin{array}{ccc}\cos 3 \theta+8 & -4 & -3 \\ \cos 2 \theta-3 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right|=0\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & 2 \cos 3 \theta+16+4 \cos 2 \theta-12=0 \\ & \left(4 \cos ^3 \theta-3 \cos \theta\right)+2\left(2 \cos ^2 \theta-1\right)+2=0 \\ & 4 \cos ^3 \theta+4 \cos ^2 \theta-3 \cos \theta=0 \\ & \cos \theta\left(4 \cos ^2 \theta+4 \cos \theta-3\right)=0 \\ & \cos \theta(2 \cos \theta+3)(2 \cos \theta-1)=0 \\ & \cos \theta=0, \frac{1}{2},-\frac{3}{2} \text { (rejected) } \\ & \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{5 \pi}{3} \\ & \text { Sum }=4 \pi\end{aligned}$

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 9 & 3 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{B}=\left[\mathrm{b}_{i j}\right], 1 \leq i, j \leq 3$. If $\mathrm{B}=\mathrm{A}^{99}-\mathrm{I}$, then the value of $\frac{\mathrm{b}_{31}-\mathrm{b}_{21}}{\mathrm{~b}_{32}}$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{P}+\mathrm{I} \\ & \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}0 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 9 & 3 & 0\end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right] \\ & \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{P}+\mathrm{I} \text { where } \mathrm{P}^3=0 \\ & \mathrm{~A}^{99}=\mathrm{I}+99 \mathrm{P}+{ }^{99} \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{P}^2\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}^{99}-\mathrm{I}=99\left[\begin{array}{lll}0 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 9 & 3 & 0\end{array}\right]+{ }^{99} \mathrm{C}_2\left[\begin{array}{lll}0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 9 & 0 & 0\end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 0 & 0 \\ 99 \times 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 44550 & 99 \times 3 & 0\end{array}\right]\end{aligned}$

$b_{31}=44550, b_{21}=99 \times 3, b_{32}=99 \times 3$

$$\therefore $$ $\frac{\mathrm{b}_{31}-\mathrm{b}_{21}}{\mathrm{~b}_{32}}$ = $\frac{44550-297}{297}=149$

questions.questionText: A building has ground floor and 10 more floors. Nine persons enter in a lift at the ground floor. The lift goes up to the $10^{\text {th }}$ floor. The number of ways, in which any 4 persons exit at a floor and the remaining 5 persons exit at a different floor, if the lift does not stop at the first and the second floors, is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
First, decide which $4$ out of the $9$ persons will get down together. The remaining $5$ persons are then fixed automatically.

Number of ways to form the two groups of sizes $4$ and $5$ is $$\frac{9!}{4!5!}=126$$

Now, the lift does not stop at the $1^{\text{st}}$ and $2^{\text{nd}}$ floors. So people can get down only on floors $3$ to $10$, i.e., $8$ possible floors.

Choose any $2$ different floors out of these $8$ floors for the two groups to exit: $${ }^8 \mathrm{C}_2=28$$

After choosing the two floors, we must decide which floor is for the group of $4$ and which floor is for the group of $5$. This can be done in $2$ ways.

So, total number of ways is $$126 \times 28 \times 2=7056$$

questions.questionText: A bag contains 6 blue and 6 green balls. Pairs of balls are drawn without replacement until the bag is empty. The probability that each drawn pair consists of one blue and one green ball is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We want every pair drawn to have one blue and one green. Since there are 6 blue and 6 green balls, we will draw a total of 6 pairs.

For each pair to be mixed (one of each colour), we must match the 6 blue balls with the 6 green balls in pairs. The number of such “good” pairings is counted by choosing and matching one ball of one colour with one ball of the other colour.

The total number of ways to split 12 balls into 6 pairs (without caring about the order of drawing inside a pair) is counted separately. The required probability is:

Probability $=\frac{\lfloor 6 \times\lfloor 6}{\left(\frac{12}{\left(\lfloor 2)^6\lfloor 6\right.}\right) \times\lfloor 6}=\frac{16}{231}$

So, the probability that each drawn pair consists of one blue and one green ball is $\frac{16}{231}$.

questions.questionText: Let $\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{(\tan (x-2))\left(\mathrm{r} x^2+(\mathrm{p}-2) x-2 \mathrm{p}\right)}{(x-2)^2}=5$ for some $\mathrm{r}, \mathrm{p} \in \boldsymbol{R}$. If the set of all possible values of q , such that the roots of the equation $\mathrm{r} x^2-\mathrm{p} x+\mathrm{q}=0$ lie in $(0,2)$, be the interval $(\alpha, \beta]$, then $4(\alpha+\beta)$ equals :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We are given

$$ \lim_{x \to 2} \frac{\tan(x-2)\left(rx^2+(p-2)x-2p\right)}{(x-2)^2}=5. $$

We have to first find the relation between $r$ and $p$, and then determine all values of $q$ for which the roots of

$$ rx^2-px+q=0 $$

lie in $(0,2)$.

Step 1: Simplify the limit

Let

$$ f(x)=rx^2+(p-2)x-2p. $$

Then the given limit becomes

$$ \lim_{x\to 2}\frac{\tan(x-2)\,f(x)}{(x-2)^2}=5. $$

Now, as $x\to 2$,

$$ \tan(x-2)\sim x-2. $$

So the limit becomes

$$ \lim_{x\to 2}\frac{(x-2)f(x)}{(x-2)^2} = \lim_{x\to 2}\frac{f(x)}{x-2}. $$

For this limit to be finite, we must have

$$ f(2)=0. $$

Now,

$$ f(2)=r(2)^2+(p-2)(2)-2p=4r+2p-4-2p=4r-4. $$

So,

$$ 4r-4=0 \implies r=1. $$

Then

$$ f(x)=x^2+(p-2)x-2p. $$

Since $f(2)=0$, factor $(x-2)$:

$$ x^2+(p-2)x-2p=(x-2)(x+p). $$

Hence the limit becomes

$$ \lim_{x\to 2}\frac{\tan(x-2)(x-2)(x+p)}{(x-2)^2} = \lim_{x\to 2}\frac{\tan(x-2)}{x-2}(x+p). $$

Using

$$ \lim_{t\to 0}\frac{\tan t}{t}=1, $$

we get

$$ 1\cdot (2+p)=5. $$

So,

$$ p+2=5 \implies p=3. $$

Thus,

$$ r=1,\qquad p=3. $$

Step 2: Form the quadratic equation

Now the equation becomes

$$ x^2-3x+q=0. $$

We want both roots to lie in $(0,2)$.

Let the roots be $\alpha_1,\alpha_2$. Then

$$ \alpha_1+\alpha_2=3,\qquad \alpha_1\alpha_2=q. $$

Since both roots lie in $(0,2)$, let us find the condition on $q$.

Step 3: Use condition for both roots in $(0,2)$

Let

$$ f(x)=x^2-3x+q. $$

Since coefficient of $x^2$ is positive, parabola opens upward.

For both roots to lie in $(0,2)$, we need:

1. Real roots

So discriminant must be non-negative:

$$ \Delta=9-4q\ge 0 \implies q\le \frac94. $$

2. Both roots positive

Since product of roots is $q$,

$$ q>0. $$

3. Both roots less than $2$

Now check value at $x=2$:

$$ f(2)=4-6+q=q-2. $$

If both roots are less than $2$, then for an upward parabola, point $x=2$ lies to the right of both roots, so we need

$$ f(2)>0. $$

Thus,

$$ q-2>0 \implies q>2. $$

Now combine:

$$ 2

So the set of all possible values of $q$ is

$$ (\alpha,\beta]=(2,\tfrac94]. $$

Hence,

$$ \alpha=2,\qquad \beta=\frac94. $$

Therefore,

$$ 4(\alpha+\beta)=4\left(2+\frac94\right) =4\left(\frac{17}{4}\right)=17. $$

Final Answer

$$ \boxed{17} $$

So, the correct option is Option C.

questions.questionText: Consider the quadratic equation $\left(n^2-2 n+2\right) x^2-3 x+\left(n^2-2 n+2\right)^2=0, n \in \mathbf{R}$. Let $\alpha$ be the minimum value of the product of its roots and $\beta$ be the maximum value of the sum of its roots. Then the sum of the first six terms of the G.P., whose first term is $\alpha$ and the common ratio is $\frac{\alpha}{\beta}$, is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For the quadratic equation

$$ \left(n^2-2n+2\right)x^2-3x+\left(n^2-2n+2\right)^2=0, $$

let us first compare it with the standard quadratic form

$$ ax^2+bx+c=0. $$

So,

$$ a=n^2-2n+2,\qquad b=-3,\qquad c=\left(n^2-2n+2\right)^2. $$

Now rewrite $a$ in a simpler form:

$$ n^2-2n+2=(n-1)^2+1. $$

Hence,

$$ a=(n-1)^2+1. $$

Since $(n-1)^2 \ge 0$, we get

$$ a \ge 1. $$

Now, for a quadratic equation, by Vieta’s formulas:

  • Sum of roots $= -\dfrac{b}{a}$

  • Product of roots $= \dfrac{c}{a}$

So here,

$$ \text{Sum of roots}=\frac{3}{a} $$

and

$$ \text{Product of roots}=\frac{a^2}{a}=a. $$

Therefore,

$$ \text{Product of roots}=(n-1)^2+1. $$

So the minimum value of the product is clearly

$$ \alpha=1, $$

attained when $n=1$.

Now the sum of roots is

$$ \frac{3}{(n-1)^2+1}. $$

Since the denominator is minimum when $(n-1)^2=0$, the maximum value of the sum is

$$ \beta=\frac{3}{1}=3. $$

So we have

$$ \alpha=1,\qquad \beta=3. $$

Now the G.P. has

  • first term $a=\alpha=1$

  • common ratio $r=\dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}=\dfrac{1}{3}$

We need the sum of first six terms:

$$ S_6=a\cdot \frac{1-r^6}{1-r}. $$

Substitute the values:

$$ S_6=1\cdot \frac{1-\left(\frac13\right)^6}{1-\frac13}. $$

Now,

$$ \left(\frac13\right)^6=\frac{1}{729} $$

and

$$ 1-\frac13=\frac23. $$

Hence,

$$ S_6=\frac{1-\frac{1}{729}}{\frac23} =\frac{\frac{728}{729}}{\frac23} =\frac{728}{729}\cdot \frac32. $$

So,

$$ S_6=\frac{1092}{729} =\frac{364}{243}. $$

Therefore, the required sum is

$$ \boxed{\frac{364}{243}}. $$

So the correct option is

$$ \boxed{\text{Option C}}. $$

questions.questionText: The sum $1+\frac{1}{2}\left(1^2+2^2\right)+\frac{1}{3}\left(1^2+2^2+3^2\right)+\ldots$ upto 10 terms is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}\left(1^2+2^2+\ldots+\mathrm{n}^2\right) \\ & \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{6 \mathrm{n}} \\ & \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{6}\left[2 \mathrm{n}^2+3 \mathrm{n}+1\right] \\ & \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}=\Sigma \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{6}\left[2 \Sigma \mathrm{n}^2+3 \Sigma \mathrm{n}+\Sigma 1\right] \\ & \Sigma \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{6}\left[2 \cdot \frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{6}+\frac{3 \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}+\mathrm{n}\right] \\ & \mathrm{S}_{10}=\frac{1}{6}\left[\frac{2 \times 10 \times 11 \times 21}{6}+\frac{3}{2} \times 10 \times 11+10\right] \\ & =\frac{10}{6}\left[77+\frac{33}{2}+1\right]=\frac{315}{2}\end{aligned}$

questions.questionText: Let the mean and the variance of seven observations $2,4, \alpha, 8, \beta, 12,14, \alpha<\beta$, be 8 and 16 respectively. Then the quadratic equation whose roots are $3 \alpha+2$ and $2 \beta+1$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
To find the required quadratic equation, we must first find the values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

Given that there are 7 observations: $2, 4, \alpha, 8, \beta, 12, 14$. Let $n = 7$.

The mean of these observations ($\bar{x}$) is $8$.

According to the formula for the mean:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} $$

Substituting the given values:

$$ 8 = \frac{2 + 4 + \alpha + 8 + \beta + 12 + 14}{7} $$

$$ 56 = 40 + \alpha + \beta $$

$$ \alpha + \beta = 16 \quad \text{--- (1)} $$

Next, the variance ($\sigma^2$) is given as $16$. The formula for variance is:

$$ \sigma^2 = \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - (\bar{x})^2 $$

Substituting the known values:

$$ 16 = \frac{2^2 + 4^2 + \alpha^2 + 8^2 + \beta^2 + 12^2 + 14^2}{7} - (8)^2 $$

$$ 16 = \frac{4 + 16 + \alpha^2 + 64 + \beta^2 + 144 + 196}{7} - 64 $$

$$ 16 + 64 = \frac{424 + \alpha^2 + \beta^2}{7} $$

$$ 80 \times 7 = 424 + \alpha^2 + \beta^2 $$

$$ 560 = 424 + \alpha^2 + \beta^2 $$

$$ \alpha^2 + \beta^2 = 136 \quad \text{--- (2)} $$

Now, using the algebraic identity $(\alpha + \beta)^2 = \alpha^2 + \beta^2 + 2\alpha \beta $, we can find the product $\alpha \beta$:

$$ (16)^2 = 136 + 2\alpha \beta $$

$$ 256 = 136 + 2\alpha \beta $$

$$ 2\alpha \beta = 120 $$

$$ \alpha \beta = 60 $$

The numbers $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of the quadratic equation $t^2 - (\alpha + \beta)t + \alpha \beta = 0$:

$$ t^2 - 16t + 60 = 0 $$

$$ t^2 - 10t - 6t + 60 = 0 $$

$$ t(t - 10) - 6(t - 10) = 0 $$

$$ (t - 6)(t - 10) = 0 $$

Thus, the values are $6$ and $10$. Since we are given that $\alpha < \beta$, we have:

$$ \alpha = 6 $$

$$ \beta = 10 $$

We are asked to find the quadratic equation whose roots are $3\alpha + 2$ and $2\beta + 1$. Let's evaluate these roots:

$$ \text{First root} = 3(6) + 2 = 18 + 2 = 20 $$

$$ \text{Second root} = 2(10) + 1 = 20 + 1 = 21 $$

Now, let's find the sum and the product of the roots for our new quadratic equation:

$$ \text{Sum of roots} = 20 + 21 = 41 $$

$$ \text{Product of roots} = 20 \times 21 = 420 $$

The standard form of a quadratic equation is $x^2 - (\text{Sum of roots})x + (\text{Product of roots}) = 0$.

Substituting the values:

$$ x^2 - 41x + 420 = 0 $$

Hence, the correct option is Option B.

questions.questionText: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$. Then the square of the area of the triangle with adjacent sides determined by the vectors $(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b})$ and $(\vec{a}-\vec{b})$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Given

$$ \vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}, \qquad \vec{b} = 6\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 3\hat{k} $$

We need the square of the area of the triangle whose adjacent sides are

$$ 2\vec{a} + 3\vec{b} \quad \text{and} \quad \vec{a} - \vec{b} $$

For two adjacent sides $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$, area of triangle is

$$ \frac{1}{2} |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}| $$

So, square of the area is

$$ \left( \frac{1}{2} |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}| \right)^2 = \frac{1}{4} |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}|^2 $$

Let

$$ \vec{u} = 2\vec{a} + 3\vec{b}, \qquad \vec{v} = \vec{a} - \vec{b} $$

First find $\vec{u}$:

$$ 2\vec{a} = 2(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j}+3\hat{k}) = 4\hat{i}+6\hat{j}+6\hat{k} $$

$$ 3\vec{b} = 3(6\hat{i}+3\hat{j}+3\hat{k}) = 18\hat{i}+9\hat{j}+9\hat{k} $$

Therefore,

$$ \vec{u} = 2\vec{a}+3\vec{b} = 22\hat{i}+15\hat{j}+15\hat{k} $$

Now find $\vec{v}$:

$$ \vec{v} = \vec{a}-\vec{b} = (2-6)\hat{i} + (3-3)\hat{j} + (3-3)\hat{k} = -4\hat{i} $$

Now compute $\vec{u} \times \vec{v}$:

$$ \vec{u} \times \vec{v} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 22 & 15 & 15 \\ -4 & 0 & 0 \end{vmatrix} $$

Expanding,

$$ \vec{u} \times \vec{v} = \hat{i}(15\cdot 0 - 15\cdot 0) - \hat{j}(22\cdot 0 - 15\cdot (-4)) + \hat{k}(22\cdot 0 - 15\cdot (-4)) $$

$$ = 0\hat{i} - 60\hat{j} + 60\hat{k} $$

So,

$$ |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}|^2 = 0^2 + (-60)^2 + 60^2 = 3600 + 3600 = 7200 $$

Hence square of the area of the triangle is

$$ \frac{1}{4} \times 7200 = 1800 $$

Therefore, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{1800} $$

So, Option C is correct.

questions.questionText: Let the image of the point $\mathrm{P}(0,-5,0)$ in the line $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z+1}{-2}$ be the point R and the image of the point $\mathrm{Q}\left(0, \frac{-1}{2}, 0\right)$ in the line $\frac{x-1}{-1}=\frac{y+9}{4}=\frac{z+1}{1}$ be the point S . Then the square of the area of the parallelogram PQRS is $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let us find the reflected points $R$ and $S$, and then use vectors to get the area of parallelogram $PQRS$.

1. Reflection of $P(0,-5,0)$ in the line

$ \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z+1}{-2} $

Write the line in parametric form:

$ x=1+2t,\quad y=t,\quad z=-1-2t $

So, a point on the line is

$ A(1,0,-1) $

and its direction vector is

$ \vec d_1=(2,1,-2) $

To reflect point $P$ in the line, first find the foot of perpendicular from $P$ to the line.

Let the foot be

$ M=A+t\vec d_1=(1+2t,\ t,\ -1-2t) $

Since $PM\perp \vec d_1$,

$ (\vec{PM})\cdot \vec d_1=0 $

Now,

$ \vec{PM}=M-P=(1+2t,\ t+5,\ -1-2t) $

So,

$ (1+2t,\,t+5,\,-1-2t)\cdot (2,1,-2)=0 $

$ 2(1+2t)+(t+5)+(-2)(-1-2t)=0 $

$ 2+4t+t+5+2+4t=0 $

$ 9+9t=0 $

$ t=-1 $

Hence,

$ M=(1+2(-1),\ -1,\ -1-2(-1))=(-1,-1,1) $

Since $M$ is the midpoint of $PR$,

$ R=2M-P $

$ R=2(-1,-1,1)-(0,-5,0)=(-2,3,2) $

So,

$ R=(-2,3,2) $

2. Reflection of $Q\left(0,-\frac12,0\right)$ in the line

$ \frac{x-1}{-1}=\frac{y+9}{4}=\frac{z+1}{1} $

Write the line in parametric form:

$ x=1-u,\quad y=-9+4u,\quad z=-1+u $

So, a point on the line is

$ B(1,-9,-1) $

and direction vector is

$ \vec d_2=(-1,4,1) $

Let the foot of perpendicular from $Q$ to this line be

$ N=B+u\vec d_2=(1-u,\ -9+4u,\ -1+u) $

Since $QN\perp \vec d_2$,

$ (\vec{QN})\cdot \vec d_2=0 $

Now,

$ \vec{QN}=N-Q=\left(1-u,\ -9+4u+\frac12,\ -1+u\right) =\left(1-u,\ -\frac{17}{2}+4u,\ -1+u\right) $

So,

$ \left(1-u,\ -\frac{17}{2}+4u,\ -1+u\right)\cdot (-1,4,1)=0 $

$ -(1-u)+4\left(-\frac{17}{2}+4u\right)+(-1+u)=0 $

$ -1+u-34+16u-1+u=0 $

$ 18u-36=0 $

$ u=2 $

Hence,

$ N=(1-2,\ -9+8,\ -1+2)=(-1,-1,1) $

Again, $N$ is the midpoint of $QS$, so

$ S=2N-Q $

$ S=2(-1,-1,1)-\left(0,-\frac12,0\right) =\left(-2,-2+\frac12,2\right) =\left(-2,-\frac32,2\right) $

So,

$ S=\left(-2,-\frac32,2\right) $

3. Check the parallelogram sides

Now,

$ P=(0,-5,0),\quad Q=\left(0,-\frac12,0\right),\quad R=(-2,3,2),\quad S=\left(-2,-\frac32,2\right) $

Take adjacent sides from $P$:

$ \vec{PQ}=Q-P=\left(0,\frac{9}{2},0\right) $

and

$ \vec{PR}=R-P=(-2,8,2) $

Also,

$ \vec{QS}=S-Q=(-2,-1,2) $

which is not equal to $\vec{PR}$, so let us check the correct order of parallelogram.

Observe:

$ \vec{PS}=S-P=\left(-2,\frac72,2\right) $

and

$ \vec{QR}=R-Q=\left(-2,\frac72,2\right) $

Thus, $PS\parallel QR$.

Also,

$ \vec{PQ}=\left(0,\frac92,0\right),\quad \vec{SR}=R-S=\left(0,\frac92,0\right) $

So the correct parallelogram order is $PQR S$, with adjacent sides

$ \vec{PQ}\quad \text{and} \quad \vec{PS} $

Hence area of parallelogram is

$ \left|\vec{PQ}\times \vec{PS}\right| $

4. Compute cross product

$ \vec{PQ}=\left(0,\frac92,0\right),\qquad \vec{PS}=\left(-2,\frac72,2\right) $

$ \vec{PQ}\times \vec{PS} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat i & \hat j & \hat k\\ 0 & \frac92 & 0\\ -2 & \frac72 & 2 \end{vmatrix} $

$ = \hat i\left(\frac92\cdot 2-0\cdot \frac72\right) -\hat j(0\cdot 2-0\cdot (-2)) +\hat k\left(0\cdot \frac72-\frac92\cdot (-2)\right) $

$ =9\hat i+0\hat j+9\hat k $

So,

$ \left|\vec{PQ}\times \vec{PS}\right| =\sqrt{9^2+9^2} =\sqrt{162} =9\sqrt2 $

Therefore, the square of the area is

$ (9\sqrt2)^2=162 $

$ \boxed{162} $

questions.questionText: If $\left(1-x^3\right)^{10}=\sum\limits_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{10} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{r}} x^{\mathrm{r}}(1-x)^{30-2 \mathrm{r}}$, then $\frac{9 \mathrm{a}_9}{\mathrm{a}_{10}}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .

questions.questionText: Let the line $x-y=4$ intersect the circle $\mathrm{C}:(x-4)^2+(y+3)^2=9$ at the points Q and R . If $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, \beta)$ is a point on C such that $\mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{PR}$, then $(6 \alpha+8 \beta)^2$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Since $PQ = PR$, the point $P$ must lie on the perpendicular bisector of the chord $QR$.

Now, the points $Q$ and $R$ are the intersection points of the line

$$ x-y=4 $$

with the circle

$$ (x-4)^2+(y+3)^2=9. $$

So, $QR$ is a chord of the circle lying on the line $x-y=4$.

For a circle, the perpendicular bisector of any chord passes through the centre.

The centre of the circle is

$$ (4,-3). $$

The slope of the line $x-y=4$ is $1$, so the slope of the perpendicular bisector is $-1$.

Hence the perpendicular bisector of chord $QR$ passing through $(4,-3)$ is

$$ y+3=-1(x-4) $$

$$ y+3=-x+4 $$

$$ x+y=1. $$

Therefore, point $P(\alpha,\beta)$ lies on both:

  1. the circle:

$$ (\alpha-4)^2+(\beta+3)^2=9 $$

  1. the line:

$$ \alpha+\beta=1. $$

Now we need $(6\alpha+8\beta)^2$.

Using $\alpha+\beta=1$, let

$$ \beta=1-\alpha. $$

Substitute into the circle:

$$ (\alpha-4)^2+((1-\alpha)+3)^2=9 $$

$$ (\alpha-4)^2+(4-\alpha)^2=9 $$

Since $(4-\alpha)^2=(\alpha-4)^2$,

$$ 2(\alpha-4)^2=9 $$

$$ (\alpha-4)^2=\frac{9}{2}. $$

Now,

$$ 6\alpha+8\beta=6\alpha+8(1-\alpha)=8-2\alpha. $$

So,

$$ (6\alpha+8\beta)^2=(8-2\alpha)^2=4(\alpha-4)^2. $$

Using $(\alpha-4)^2=\frac{9}{2}$,

$$ (6\alpha+8\beta)^2=4\cdot \frac{9}{2}=18. $$

Hence, the required value is

$$ \boxed{18}. $$

questions.questionText:
Let $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}x^3+8 ; & x<0, \\ x^2-4 ; & x \geq 0,\end{array}\right.$ and $g(x)= \begin{cases}(x-8)^{1 / 3} ; & x<0, \\ (x+4)^{1 / 2} ; & x \geq 0 .\end{cases}$

Then the number of points, where the function $g \circ f$ is discontinuous, is $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We need to find the points where the composite function $g \circ f$ is discontinuous.

That means we first find

$$ (g \circ f)(x)=g(f(x)). $$

So we must see, for each value of $x$, which branch of $g$ will be used.

1. Given functions

$$ f(x)= \begin{cases} x^3+8, & x<0,\\ x^2-4, & x\ge 0 \end{cases} $$

and

$$ g(x)= \begin{cases} (x-8)^{1/3}, & x<0,\\ (x+4)^{1/2}, & x\ge 0. \end{cases} $$

Now,

$$ (g\circ f)(x)=g(f(x)). $$

The definition of $g$ depends on whether its input is negative or non-negative. So we must check the sign of $f(x)$.

2. Find where $f(x)<0$ and where $f(x)\ge 0$

Case 1: $x<0$

Then

$$ f(x)=x^3+8. $$

We check when this is negative:

$$ x^3+8<0 \implies x^3<-8 \implies x<-2. $$

So for $x<0$:

  • if $x<-2$, then $f(x)<0$,

  • if $-2\le x<0$, then $f(x)\ge 0$.

Case 2: $x\ge 0$

Then

$$ f(x)=x^2-4. $$

We check when this is negative:

$$ x^2-4<0 \implies x^2<4 \implies -2

But here $x\ge 0$, so this becomes

$$ 0\le x<2. $$

Thus for $x\ge 0$:

  • if $0\le x<2$, then $f(x)<0$,

  • if $x\ge 2$, then $f(x)\ge 0$.

3. Write $(g\circ f)(x)$ piecewise

Now use the correct branch of $g$.

(i) When $x<-2$

Here $f(x)<0$, so use

$$ g(y)=(y-8)^{1/3}. $$

Thus

$$ (g\circ f)(x)=\big((x^3+8)-8\big)^{1/3}=(x^3)^{1/3}=x. $$

So for $x<-2$,

$$ (g\circ f)(x)=x. $$

(ii) When $-2\le x<0$

Here $f(x)\ge 0$, so use

$$ g(y)=\sqrt{y+4}. $$

Thus

$$ (g\circ f)(x)=\sqrt{(x^3+8)+4}=\sqrt{x^3+12}. $$

So for $-2\le x<0$,

$$ (g\circ f)(x)=\sqrt{x^3+12}. $$

(iii) When $0\le x<2$

Here $f(x)<0$, so use

$$ g(y)=(y-8)^{1/3}. $$

Thus

$$ (g\circ f)(x)=\big((x^2-4)-8\big)^{1/3}=(x^2-12)^{1/3}. $$

So for $0\le x<2$,

$$ (g\circ f)(x)=(x^2-12)^{1/3}. $$

(iv) When $x\ge 2$

Here $f(x)\ge 0$, so use

$$ g(y)=\sqrt{y+4}. $$

Thus

$$ (g\circ f)(x)=\sqrt{(x^2-4)+4}=\sqrt{x^2}=x $$

since $x\ge 2$.

So for $x\ge 2$,

$$ (g\circ f)(x)=x. $$

4. Check continuity at possible boundary points

Each expression is continuous in its own interval. So we only check the joining points:

$$ x=-2,\quad x=0,\quad x=2. $$

At $x=-2$

Left-hand limit:

for $x<-2$, $(g\circ f)(x)=x$, so

$$ \lim_{x\to -2^-}(g\circ f)(x)=-2. $$

Right-hand limit:

for $-2\le x<0$, $(g\circ f)(x)=\sqrt{x^3+12}$, so

$$ \lim_{x\to -2^+}(g\circ f)(x)=\sqrt{(-2)^3+12}=\sqrt{4}=2. $$

Since

$$ -2\ne 2, $$

the function is discontinuous at $x=-2$.

At $x=0$

Left-hand limit:

$$ \lim_{x\to 0^-}(g\circ f)(x)=\sqrt{0^3+12}=\sqrt{12}. $$

Right-hand limit:

$$ \lim_{x\to 0^+}(g\circ f)(x)=(0^2-12)^{1/3}=(-12)^{1/3}. $$

These are not equal, so discontinuous at $x=0$.

At $x=2$

Left-hand limit:

$$ \lim_{x\to 2^-}(g\circ f)(x)=(2^2-12)^{1/3}=(-8)^{1/3}=-2. $$

Right-hand limit:

$$ \lim_{x\to 2^+}(g\circ f)(x)=2. $$

These are not equal, so discontinuous at $x=2$.

5. Conclusion

The function $g\circ f$ is discontinuous at

$$ x=-2,\quad x=0,\quad x=2. $$

Hence, the number of points of discontinuity is

$$ \boxed{3}. $$

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{R}=\left\{(x, y) \in \mathbf{N} \times \mathbf{N}: \log _{\mathrm{e}}(x+y) \leq 2\right\}$. Then the minimum number of elements, required to be added in $R$ to make it a transitive relation, is $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We have

$$ R=\{(x,y)\in \mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N}:\ln(x+y)\le 2\}. $$

To make the relation transitive, we first find all pairs already in $R$.

Since

$$ \ln(x+y)\le 2 \iff x+y\le e^2. $$

Now,

$$ e^2 \approx 7.389. $$

So, for natural numbers $x,y$, we must have

$$ x+y\le 7. $$

Hence,

$$ R=\{(x,y)\in \mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N}:x+y\le 7\}. $$

Now list all such pairs:

  • For $x=1$: $(1,1),(1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(1,5),(1,6)$

  • For $x=2$: $(2,1),(2,2),(2,3),(2,4),(2,5)$

  • For $x=3$: $(3,1),(3,2),(3,3),(3,4)$

  • For $x=4$: $(4,1),(4,2),(4,3)$

  • For $x=5$: $(5,1),(5,2)$

  • For $x=6$: $(6,1)$

So these are the elements of $R$.

A relation is transitive if whenever

$$ (a,b)\in R \quad \text{and} \quad (b,c)\in R, $$

then

$$ (a,c)\in R. $$

We need to find all pairs $(a,c)$ that must be present because of transitivity.

Step 1: Find which middle elements are possible

If $(a,b)\in R$, then

$$ a+b\le 7. $$

If $(b,c)\in R$, then

$$ b+c\le 7. $$

For a fixed $b$, this gives

$$ a\le 7-b,\qquad c\le 7-b. $$

So for each $b$, transitivity demands that all pairs

$$ (a,c)\quad \text{with } 1\le a\le 7-b,\; 1\le c\le 7-b $$

must be in the relation.

Taking union over all possible $b\in \mathbb{N}$ with at least one such pair, we get:

  • For $b=1$: required pairs are all

    $$ (a,c),\quad 1\le a\le 6,\; 1\le c\le 6. $$

This is already the largest possible set forced by transitivity.

So, if relation is to be transitive, it must contain every pair

$$ (a,c)\in \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}\times \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}. $$

That means the transitive closure must at least contain all $36$ pairs

$$ (a,c),\quad 1\le a,c\le 6. $$

Step 2: Count how many of these are already in $R$

Among these $36$ pairs, $R$ already contains those with

$$ a+c\le 7. $$

Count them:

  • $a=1$: $c=1$ to $6$ $\Rightarrow 6$

  • $a=2$: $c=1$ to $5$ $\Rightarrow 5$

  • $a=3$: $c=1$ to $4$ $\Rightarrow 4$

  • $a=4$: $c=1$ to $3$ $\Rightarrow 3$

  • $a=5$: $c=1$ to $2$ $\Rightarrow 2$

  • $a=6$: $c=1$ $\Rightarrow 1$

Total:

$$ 6+5+4+3+2+1=21. $$

So the number of additional pairs needed is

$$ 36-21=15. $$

Step 3: Check that this is sufficient

If we add all missing pairs among

$$ \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}\times \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}, $$

then the relation becomes

$$ \{(a,c):1\le a\le 6,\;1\le c\le 6\}. $$

This is clearly transitive, because whenever

$$ (a,b)\in R \quad \text{and} \quad (b,c)\in R, $$

all three numbers lie in $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$, so $(a,c)$ is also in the relation.

Therefore, the minimum number of elements to be added is

$$ \boxed{15} $$
